1: <header>Environmental impact of milk production across an intensification gradient in Ethiopia </header>
Daniel Woldegebriel ² Henk Udo , Theo Vietsa, Eugenie van der Harstb, José Pottingb,c,d

<list>a Animal Production Systems Group, Wageningen University, PO Box 338, 6700 AH Wageningen, The Netherlands 
b Environmental Systems Analysis Group, Wageningen University, PO Box 47, 6700 AA Wageningen, The Netherlands 
C KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Brinellvägen 8, 114 28 Stockholm, Sweden 
d PBL Netherlands Environmental Assessment Agency, PO Box 30314, 2500 GH Den Haag, The Netherlands</list>

A R T I C INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Intensification Dairying LCA Environmental impact Tigray|This paper quantifies environmental performances of milk production systems differing in degree of in- tensification in the Mekelle milkshed area, Ethiopia. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology was used to estimate Land Use (LU), Fossil Energy Use (FEU) and Global Warming Potential (GWP) of the cattle sub-system in 8 large-scale, 8 (peri-)urban and 8 rural farms. The large-scale farms owned considerably more and other types of cattle (35.0 cattle units (cu); mainly Friesians) than the (peri-)urban (6.3 cu; mainly crossbreds) and rural farms (4.1 cu; mainly local breeds). The milk production per average cow per year was much lower in rural farms (730 kg) than in large-scale (2377 kg) and (peri-)urban farms (1829 kg). Milk was the main contributor to the economic benefits of the large-scale (90%) and (peri-)urban (80%) farms, whereas milk (sold and consumed at home) contributed only about 40% to the economic benefits in the multifunctional rural farms. The environ- mental impacts per cu, reflecting the absolute impacts of cattle keeping, were considerably higher in the large- scale and (peri-)urban farms than in the rural farms. LU and FEU were for the great majority caused by the land use for hay, straws and grasses, and harvesting, transport and processing of feeds, in particular wheat bran. On- farm emissions from enteric fermentation and manure storage were the main contributors to GWP. The impacts per kg milk did not differ significantly between the three systems. The LU per kg milk estimates (9.4, 11.2 and 8.8 m² in the large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms, respectively) were relatively high compared to LCA studies of milk production in developed countries due to large amounts of low-quality forages and wheat bran fed, whereas the FEU values per kg milk (7.5, 11.1 and 6.6 MJ in the large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms, respectively) were relatively low compared to studies of milk production systems in developed countries. The GWP estimates per kg milk (1.75, 2.25 and 2.22 kg CO2-equivalents per kg milk in the large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms, respectively) were slightly higher than GWP values for the same types of farms in other de- veloping countries, due to the relatively large amounts of low quality feeds fed. The quality of cattle manage- ment practices seems more important than the choice for a specific cattle keeping system in reducing en- vironmental impacts of milk production.
2: A large volume of explorative studies at regional and whole con- tinent levels indicates that environmental impacts per unit animal source food are highest in livestock systems in developing regions and lowest in large-scale systems in developed regions (e.g. Steinfeld et al., 2006; FAO, 2010; Herrero et al., 2013; Gerber et al., 2013). The before- cited studies propose intensification through improving feed qualities, using more productive breeds, improving management practices, and SO reducing animal numbers to produce the same amount of animal source food, to mitigate detrimental effects of livestock on the environment, in

In developing countries, large numbers of smallholders keep live- stock in support of their livelihoods. The recommendations to reduce the environmental impact of their livestock are also expected to im- prove their livelihoods (Steinfeld et al., 2006; Herrero et al., 2013; Gerber et al., 2013). However, smallholder farmers often lack sufficient capital, land, labour or feed resources for the intensification practices, or these practices and keeping less animals do not fit their sociocultural reality (Owen et al., 2011; Herrero et al., 2015; Udo et al., 2016). A relatively successful intensification strategy for smallholders is dairying based on European breeds or crossbreds. It contributes to increased

household incomes and an increased market share for smallholder milk producers, although it does not match the resources of the poorest rural households (McDermott et al., 2010; Udo et al., 2011). Many countries promote smallholder dairy production as well as market-oriented spe- cialised dairy farms to meet the increasing demands and to reduce imports of dairy commodities.

Evidence from field studies on how intensification of dairy pro- duction in developing countries affects environmental impacts is lim- ited and contradictory. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is commonly used to evaluate the environmental impact of all processes in the life cycle of a specific animal source food. LCA results of field studies vary with the specific differences in farming practices due to ecological and socio- economic conditions and with the methodological choice as most no- tably posed by allocation of impacts to physical products only, or al- locating these impacts to all functions livestock have in local commu- nities (Weiler et al., 2014). In Peru, Bartl et al. (2011) found about 4 times as high carbon footprints per kg milk produced in roughage-only and low productive milk production systems in Andean Highlands compared to coastal more productive systems. Weiler et al. (2014) and Garg et al. (2016) found that if one allocates greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions to the various livelihoods functions of the animals, GHG emissions per kg milk in smallholder dairy systems in Kenya and India, respectively, were comparable to GHG emissions per kg milk in de- veloped regions.

Studies at local levels are needed to assess whether local in- tensification strategies contribute to improved environmental perfor- mances of livestock systems. In Sub Saharan Africa (SSA), Ethiopia is a prominent country with regard to promoting intensification of milk production to meet the increasing demand. The Ethiopian Livestock Master Plan expects a substantial increase in national milk production over the period 2015-2020 as a result of providing quality feeds, im- proved extension and health services and crossbreeding by using semen of European dairy bulls (Shapiro et al., 2015). The proposed strategies are expected to create opportunities for market-oriented specialised dairy farms (Wouters and van der Lee, 2010; Land O'Lakes, 2010).

Ethiopian cattle production systems are classified into four different categories: large-scale, urban and peri-urban (here described as (peri-) urban), rural, and pastoral systems. These systems differ in production objectives, types of animals kept, availability of feed, and land and capital resources (Redda, 2001; Yilma et al., 2011). The large-scale system involves institutional farms and private companies. Their high- grade Friesian cattle are stall-fed. Milk production is for the market. The (peri-)urban system comprises smallholders located in and around urban areas. These smallholders primarily keep crossbred cattle for milk production (Ahmed al., 2003). Milk is directly sold to consumers (Redda, 2001). Feeds are mainly purchased fodders and concentrates. Large-scale and (peri-)urban systems comprise only 2% of the total milk production (Tariku, 2006). The rural system produces the great ma- jority of the milk. It can be found in most highlands. Rural smallholders not only keep cattle to produce milk for the market but also for home consumption; added to this cattle support crop production via manure and draught power, give ready access to money when an animal is sold, and are a security for future financial needs and for asking assistance from fellow farmers when needed (Behnke and Metaferio, 2011). The rural system is dominated by local bos indicus breeds. Feeding depends on grazing communal pasture land and supplementation with crop re- sidues. The traditional pastoral cattle system is multifunctional, milk is for home consumption, and communal pasture land provides the bulk of the feed (Tonamo, 2016).

Cattle, are a main contributor to GHG emissions in Ethiopia; by means of their enteric fermentation and manure management they contribute 45% to the total methane emissions of 52 MT CO2-equiva- lents (Anonymus, 2011a). Enhancing intensification is expected to have large mitigation potential. Are more intensive dairy systems indeed beneficial for the environment? This paper quantifies environmental performances of the three production systems (large-scale, (peri-)urban

and rural systems) that produce milk for the market in the Mekelle milkshed area in Ethiopia. These systems represent different steps in intensification of dairying in Ethiopia. The pastoral system is not in- cluded in this study as it is not producing milk for the market.

Ethiopian cattle production systems are classified into four different categories: large-scale, urban and peri-urban (here described as (peri-) urban), rural, and pastoral systems. These systems differ in production objectives, types of animals kept, availability of feed, and land and capital resources (Redda, 2001; Yilma et al., 2011). The large-scale system involves institutional farms and private companies. Their high- grade Friesian cattle are stall-fed. Milk production is for the market. The (peri-)urban system comprises smallholders located in and around urban areas. These smallholders primarily keep crossbred cattle for milk production (Ahmed al., 2003). Milk is directly sold to consumers (Redda, 2001). Feeds are mainly purchased fodders and concentrates. Large-scale and (peri-)urban systems comprise only 2% of the total milk production (Tariku, 2006). The rural system produces the great ma- jority of the milk. It can be found in most highlands. Rural smallholders not only keep cattle to produce milk for the market but also for home consumption; added to this cattle support crop production via manure and draught power, give ready access to money when an animal is sold, and are a security for future financial needs and for asking assistance from fellow farmers when needed (Behnke and Metaferio, 2011). The rural system is dominated by local bos indicus breeds. Feeding depends on grazing communal pasture land and supplementation with crop re- sidues. The traditional pastoral cattle system is multifunctional, milk is for home consumption, and communal pasture land provides the bulk of the feed (Tonamo, 2016).

Cattle, are a main contributor to GHG emissions in Ethiopia; by means of their enteric fermentation and manure management they contribute 45% to the total methane emissions of 52 MT CO2-equiva- lents (Anonymus, 2011a). Enhancing intensification is expected to have large mitigation potential. Are more intensive dairy systems indeed beneficial for the environment? This paper quantifies environmental performances of the three production systems (large-scale, (peri-)urban

and rural systems) that produce milk for the market in the Mekelle milkshed area in Ethiopia. These systems represent different steps in intensification of dairying in Ethiopia. The pastoral system is not in- cluded in this study as it is not producing milk for the market.
8: This study applied the LCA methodology. An LCA starts with the specification of the goal and scope of the study which includes the definition of the system and the system boundary, the Functional Unit (FU) to which environmental impacts are allocated, the applied allo- cation principles, and the impact categories to be evaluated. Then LCA quantifies the resources consumed and the emissions to the environ- ment in a product's life cycle (inventory analysis). Next, LCA evaluates the contribution of these resource consumptions and emissions to re- levant environmental impact categories (impact assessment) (ISO, 2006).

The LCA of milk production in large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural cattle farms was based on the inventory of all processes related to cattle keeping up to farm gate, including the animals (enteric fermentation and manure storage), forages and feed supplements used, feed pro- duction and transport, and the different products of cattle keeping. Emissions of manure were assigned to the cattle sub-system of the farms during time of storage (8 months) on the farm. Thereafter, manure is used for the crop sub-system on the own farm, sold, or used as fuel.

Impact assessment was based on two FUs: a) 1 adult cattle unit (cu), and b) 1 kg of milk produced by a cow. A cow (lactating or dry) or an adult bull represented 1 cu, a heifer or young bull accounted for 0.7 cu and a calf for 0.2 cu. The environmental impacts per cu reflect the absolute impacts of keeping cattle, whereas the environmental impacts per kg milk reflect the impact of the production of milk.

The inventory analysis comprised the input and output data col- lection and processing for each activity in the cattle production system. Economic allocation was used to allocate the environmental inputs and outputs from the inventory analysis to the various outputs from the cattle based on the economic values of these outputs.

The impact categories considered were land use (LU), fossil energy use (FEU) and Global Warming Potential (GWP). LU represents the common concern that livestock systems occupy a considerable part of the terrestrial surface area and that the arable land base cannot be expanded (Yilma et al., 2011; Cook et al., 2015). LU is a main driving force behind biodiversity loss (Baan et al., 2013). FEU represents the concern that fossil energy gets exhausted, furthermore farmers face great difficulties in access to energy services (Best, 2014). GWP re- presents the general concern about climate impact of livestock through emission of greenhouse gases. The main greenhouse gases related to cattle keeping are carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). Emission of CO2 results from the combustion of fossil energy to power machinery such as generators or harvesters. CH4 re- sults mainly from enteric fermentation and manure management. N2O results mainly from manure management. Eutrophication and acid- ification as impact categories were not included in the present study; although they are widely used impact categories in LCAs of animal source foods produced in industrialised systems they were considered less relevant for the cattle keeping situation in Ethiopia. Other impact categories, such as ecotoxicity, human toxicity or ozon layer depletion are generally not included in LCAs of milk production due to limited data availability or limited impacts found in specific studies (Berlin, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Yan et al., 2011).
The study area was the Mekelle milkshed area. Mekelle is the capital of the Tigray region, located in the Ethiopian Highlands, approximately

780 km from Addis Ababa. The Tigray region is situated between 12°15' and 14°57' N and 36°27' and 39°59' E. The average annual rainfall range is 350-710 mm, with a rainy season from June to September. The altitude in the area is 1400-1800 m. Characteristic topographic features are plateaus with some rolling escarpments and flat to undulating valley floors. The Mekelle milkshed area is one of the eight milkshed areas in Ethiopia (Brandsma et al., 2013). The Mekelle milkshed covers seven out of the 36 districts in Tigray. Communities in a radius of 2-5 km along main roads, connecting Mekelle and four other major towns, are part of the milkshed. The total human population in the milkshed is one million (Brandsma et al., 2013). A milkshed area has potential to develop a formal value chain and to transform subsistence farming to commercial farming. Out of the about 14 000 adult cattle in the Mekelle milkshed area about 300 are high-grade or purebred Frie- sians, about 4300 are crossbred cattle (Friesian X local breeds), and the remainder are cattle of local breeds (Brandsma et al., 2013; Bureau of Agriculture and Rural Development of the Tigray (Tigray National Regional State) regional government, personal communication).

13: Prior to on-farm data collection an official visit to the BoARD (Bureau of Agriculture and Rural Development of the Tigray (Tigray National Regional State) regional government) was made. BoARD provided a list of the 8 large-scale and 328 (peri-)urban (located in Mekelle or in the periphery of Mekelle) dairy farms in the Mekelle milkshed area. In total, 24 farms were selected: the 8 large-scale farms, 8 randomly selected (peri-)urban farms and 8 farms randomly selected from the rural farms in Mekelle Adi-kolomay, at about 15 km from Mekelle. The large-scale and (peri-)urban farmers were contacted through a technician providing artificial insemination (AI) services. The AI technician is frequently visiting these farms and has great rapport with the farmers. This made data collection from these farms easy and reliable.

Questionnaires were used to gather data regarding household and farm characteristics and the external and internal inputs and outputs of cattle farming over the period September 2011-August 2012. The questionnaires were filled out directly by face to face interviews with the farmers and farm owners. Each farm was visited three times in the period August-September 2012, one time for communication, one for data collection and one for crosschecking the data. Each visit took about 1.5 Data concerning household composition, education, crop and pasture land, herd composition, milk yields, manure production, records of animals sold, and quantities of the different feed types (self- produced and purchased) were collected for each farm. The large-scale farms kept records of their purchased inputs and outputs, whereas in- formation of purchased inputs and outputs and home consumption for (peri-)urban and rural farmers was based on farmer recall information. The use of hired labour in the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms was recorded. Hired labour costs were estimated at 500 birr (one US$ = 17 birr) per month. In rural farms only family labour was used for cattle keeping practices. Body weights of different categories of cattle (cows, young female stock, (young) bulls) were estimated by using chest girth measurements of individual animals.

Stall-feeding was practised in the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms, whereas cattle in the rural farms grazed communal pasture areas and harvested crop fields during the daytime. The feeds offered consisted both of roughages produced within farms, such as hay, alfalfa, elephant grass, and wheat and barley straw, and purchased feeds, such as hay, straws, dairy meal, wheat bran, noug cake (residue after extraction of oil from the seeds of Guizotia abyssinica), atella (residue of home brewed alcohol, tella, based on fermentation of mixtures of different grains), peas, and mineral supplements. Data regarding the nutritional contents and mass of co-products were estimated based on available literature (Mekoya et al., 2007; Demeke, 2007; Tolera, 2008; Dejene et al., 2009; Tesfay, 2010; Shewangizaw, 2014). The dairy meal

consisted of 50% wheat bran, 48% oil cakes and 2% sodium chloride (Dejene et al., 2009). Also data on washing agents, electricity, water and diesel consumption were collected at each farm. Water and elec- tricity were collected by converting the bill per month to m³ and kWh based on the rate from the Ethiopian water supply and sanitation cor- poration and the Ethiopian electricity corporation, respectively.

The use of hired labour in the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms was recorded. Hired labour costs were estimated at 500 birr (one US$ = 17 birr) per month. In rural farms only family labour was used for cattle keeping practices. Body weights of different categories of cattle (cows, young female stock, (young) bulls) were estimated by using chest girth measurements of individual animals.

Stall-feeding was practised in the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms, whereas cattle in the rural farms grazed communal pasture areas and harvested crop fields during the daytime. The feeds offered consisted both of roughages produced within farms, such as hay, alfalfa, elephant grass, and wheat and barley straw, and purchased feeds, such as hay, straws, dairy meal, wheat bran, noug cake (residue after extraction of oil from the seeds of Guizotia abyssinica), atella (residue of home brewed alcohol, tella, based on fermentation of mixtures of different grains), peas, and mineral supplements. Data regarding the nutritional contents and mass of co-products were estimated based on available literature (Mekoya et al., 2007; Demeke, 2007; Tolera, 2008; Dejene et al., 2009; Tesfay, 2010; Shewangizaw, 2014). The dairy meal

consisted of 50% wheat bran, 48% oil cakes and 2% sodium chloride (Dejene et al., 2009). Also data on washing agents, electricity, water and diesel consumption were collected at each farm. Water and elec- tricity were collected by converting the bill per month to m³ and kWh based on the rate from the Ethiopian water supply and sanitation cor- poration and the Ethiopian electricity corporation, respectively.
16: We considered emissions under the current production conditions. Calculation of GHG emissions from enteric fermentation was based on the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) good practice guidance, Tier 2 approach (IPCC, 2006). The emissions from enteric fermentation were estimated for three animal categories: cows, young stock and (young) bulls. Numbers of animals in these categories, their body weights and if applicable milk yields were based on estimates based on collection of information on the study farms.

CH4 and N2O emissions from manure storage were calculated using methane emission factors for African farming systems and nitrous oxide emission factors both from IPCC (2006). Other inventory and emissions data such as from industrial processes were based on life cycle in- ventories in the Ecoinvent 2.2 database (available at http:/pre-sus- tainability.com) in the SimaPro 8.0 software (available at http://pre- sustainability.com). Information on electricity production in Ethiopia is not available in the Ecoinvent database. We assumed that electricity consumption in Ethiopia is for 88% produced by hydropower, 11% is from diesel and 1% is geothermal (Meder et al., 2011).

Production and transport of feed resources are also responsible for emissions and use of resources. Distances from the origin for wheat (from Australia) were estimated as: overseas transport of 10 037 km Australia to Djibouti, 1183 km within Australia and 798 km from Djibouti to Mekelle. Assumed means of transport were a transoceanic freight ship and a lorry > 16 (fleet average Europe). All local trans- port for farm inputs, such as feeds, were assumed using either a lorry > 16 t (fleet average Europe) or a lorry < 3.5 (fleet average Europe). For LU calculations life cycle inventory data from Ecoinvent 2.2 were used. Wheat bran was imported from Australia and processed from locally grown wheat. Wheat production data from Australia and Ethiopia were not available in Ecoinvent 2.2. We used wheat produc- tion data from the US available in Ecoinvent and yield data from Ethiopia (Anonymus, 2011b). For the hay, grass and straw production data from extensive production systems in Ecoinvent 2.2 were used. For peas the integrated production data from Ecoinvent 2.2 were used. Yields of elephant grass and alfalfa were not available in Ecoinvent. Yields per ha of elephant grass (15 t) and alfalfa (12.25 t) were based on Ethiopian literature (Zewdu, 2008; Anonymus, 2011b; Mulualem and Molla, 2013). The use of communal lands for grazing in the rural system could not be reliably quantified as there are many users of these lands. Therefore, we did not include the impact of the use of the communal lands in the LU estimate of the rural system.

18: Economic allocation is widely used in LCAs of livestock production systems with multiple outputs if the interest is in one particular product such as milk in this study. All three included farming systems have as tangible products milk sold and home consumed, animals sold and manure used or sold. Farm gate prices were used to estimate the eco- nomic values of the products sold and used by the family or on the land (opportunity values). Milk price per kg was 10 birr in the large-scale and (peri-)urban systems and 8 birr in the rural system. This price difference is caused by a lack of appropriate marketing facilities in the rural area. The economic value of animals sold was based on the selling prices of different categories of animals. The economic value of manure was based on the manure produced, calculated as 8 kg DM manure per 1000 kg animal live weight (IPCC, 2006) and the average price estimate

by the farmers of 214 birr per ton. It was estimated that 25% of the faeces and urine from cattle in the rural system was deposited on the grazing areas, based on the estimate of 6-7 grazing hours per day per animal. In the eight rural study farms cattle were not used for draught power or only very occasionally. In the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms cattle are never used for draught power purposes.

Cattle keeping in smallholder systems in rural areas has additional benefits varying from the insurance value of the stock on hand and the financing value of the stock sold to social status related to cattle keeping (Moll, 2005). The insurance and the financing value are im- portant in areas lacking financial institutions. The economic value from the insurance component of cattle keeping is based on the idea that the capital incorporated in a herd is a guarantee that future capital re- quirements can be met. For such a benefit, an insurance premium must be paid when an insurance market exists. The insurance value of cattle as an insurance for the household is estimated as the value of the stock on hand multiplied by an estimate of the insurance premium that farmers would have to pay for an insurance equal to the capital value of their stock (Moll et al., 2007). This insurance factor in the rural system was estimated at 10%, based on urban insurance rates. Animals are sold when the household has urgent cash needs. The economic value from financing is based on the idea of not having to pay interest for bor- rowing money from a bank or informal money lender when an animal is sold for urgent cash needs. The financing value is calculated as the total value of cattle sold in a farm per year multiplied by an estimate of the interest rate of credit (Moll et al., 2007). This financing factor in the rural system was estimated at 8%, based on commercial interest rates for short and medium term credit in Ethiopia. In rural areas cattle are a major indicator of status. A conservative estimate of the status value of cattle was based on the value of the stock on hand multiplied by a status factor of 3% (Ayalew, 2001; Moll, 2005).

When crop by-products were used as feed, economic allocation was applied based on the economic value of the crop for human food use and the value of its by-products as cattle feed. These prices were based on local market prices. Economic allocation of the environmental im- pacts to wheat bran was based on the ratio wheat bran: wheat flour of 10: 90 (Gebremedhin et al., 2009); for atella it was atella: tella 12: 88 (Tadesse and Yayneshet, 2011); for field pea hulls it was field pea hulls: field peas 1.4: 98.6 (Bogale, 2012).

The Gross Margins (GM) of cattle keeping were calculated as the money values of the tangible products and the additional benefits minus the cash inputs (including hired labour) per farm. The economic effi- ciency was calculated as the GM per cu.

22: The SimaPro software was used in the calculation of the LU, FEU and GWP impacts. SimaPro allows assessing and comparing the en- vironmental impacts of products and services. LU was expressed as m² and energy use as MJ per year for the FU's cu or kg milk. The GWP per year for the FU's cu or kg milk was calculated by summing the emissions of CO2, CH4 and N2O based on their equivalence factor in terms of CO2- equivalents: 1 for CO2, 25 for CH4 and 298 for N2O (IPCC, 2006). En- vironmental hotspots were determined as the processes that contributed most to the different environmental impact categories.
23: One-way ANOVA was performed to analyse the variation in relevant farm characteristics and in LU, FEU and GWP per animal and per kg milk among the three milk production systems. Post-hoc test was used for comparing means.

26: Table 1 presents household and farm characteristics of the large- scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms included in this study. Household size was lowest for the five privately owned large-scale farms. The other three large-scale farms were owned by development organisations. The household heads of rural farms were about nine years older than the heads of the other farms types. The five household heads of the large- scale farms had received secondary or higher education, whereas only one farm household head of the (peri-)urban farms had received sec- ondary or higher education. Four of the eight household heads of the (peri-)urban farms had received no formal education but they were able to read and write. Five of the eight rural household heads had no education at all. (Peri-)urban farmers are market-oriented. Their market is in the city centre. During the dry season when there are less farming activities some of the family members work as day labourers in the city. As could be expected, the three farming systems differed con- siderably in farm characteristics and cattle keeping practices. The large- scale farms used their land for both cropping (5.2 ha) and forage pro- duction (2.5 ha). The (peri-)urban farms owned only 0.4 ha of land. The land owned by rural farms (3.3 ha) was almost exclusively used for cropping. Only in the rural farms cattle were grazed. The number of cattle differed significantly between the farm types: the large-scale farms owned considerably more cattle (35.0 cattle units) than the (peri- )urban (6.5 cu) and rural farms (4.1 cu). Cows formed the majority of the herds (24.6, 4.9 and 1.9 cows in the large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms, respectively). The large-scale farms kept high-grade Friesian cattle, the (peri-)urban farms mainly 95%) kept crossbreds (Friesian X local breeds), whereas the rural farms mainly 95%) kept local cattle. Average body weights for COWS (lactating and dry cows), young stock (calves and heifers), and (young) bulls in the large-scale systems were 468, 230 and 218 kg, respectively; in the (peri-)urban system these were 390 and 184 kg for cows and young stock (no bulls), respectively; and in the rural system these were 231, 138 and 151 kg for cows, young stock and young bulls, respectively.

28: The main inputs for the cattle are feeds. Table 2 gives estimates of the daily amounts of Dry Matter (DM) per cu of the different feeds of- fered. Hay and straws were the major feeds offered. In the large-scale and (peri-)urban system also considerable amounts of wheat bran were

Sources CP (crude protein) percentage in DM estimates: Demeke (2007); Mekoya

fed. In the large-scale system dairy meal, alfalfa and elephant grass were other widely used feed sources. Atella was mainly fed in the (peri- )urban and rural systems. The feeds used were comparable to another study on livestock feed sources used in the Tigray region (Gebremariam and Belay, 2016). The feed refusals could not be estimated, SO amounts consumed cannot be estimated. In particular, a considerable part of the roughages used are wasted. The feed wastes are mixed with manure in order to fertilise crop fields.

The majority of the feeds offered are low in Crude Protein (CP). When we use average CP values of the feeds offered (Table 2) then the average CP percentage of the DM of the average diet offered is below 10%. A milk producing cow requires 12-18% CP in her diet, depending on the stage of lactation, whereas a dry cow requires 10-12% CP in her diet (Moran, 2005). So the feeds offered were far below requirements for milk producing cows. In reality more of the low quality feeds will not be consumed, so the actual diet consumed will be of slightly better quality than the average of the daily amounts offered. Even so, feed qualities of the actual diets will be below requirements.
30: Feeding practices of large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural cattle keeping systems in Mekelle milkshed area.

Sources CP (crude protein) percentage in DM estimates: Demeke (2007); Mekoya

fed. In the large-scale system dairy meal, alfalfa and elephant grass were other widely used feed sources. Atella was mainly fed in the (peri- )urban and rural systems. The feeds used were comparable to another study on livestock feed sources used in the Tigray region (Gebremariam and Belay, 2016). The feed refusals could not be estimated, SO amounts consumed cannot be estimated. In particular, a considerable part of the roughages used are wasted. The feed wastes are mixed with manure in order to fertilise crop fields.

The majority of the feeds offered are low in Crude Protein (CP). When we use average CP values of the feeds offered (Table 2) then the average CP percentage of the DM of the average diet offered is below 10%. A milk producing cow requires 12-18% CP in her diet, depending on the stage of lactation, whereas a dry cow requires 10-12% CP in her diet (Moran, 2005). So the feeds offered were far below requirements for milk producing cows. In reality more of the low quality feeds will not be consumed, so the actual diet consumed will be of slightly better quality than the average of the daily amounts offered. Even so, feed qualities of the actual diets will be below requirements.

32: Table 3 gives outputs of the farms. During one year, a farm was estimated to produce about 47.8, 7.2, and 3.0 of DM manure in the large-scale, (peri)urban and rural system, respectively. Farmers used the manure as fertilizer on their crop land, sold it, or used it for fuel. During one year, a farm produced 64.6, 8.9 and 1.1 t of milk in the large-scale, (peri)-urban and rural system respectively. In general, farmers sell their milk to milk traders, consumers or cafeteria. There is a milk processing unit in the area, however, this unit is not functioning on regular basis. It seems that up to now, rural farmers are only to a small extent integrated in the milk value chain; the majority (85%) of the milk produced was consumed at home. In the (peri-)urban system about 27% of the milk produced was consumed at home.

The milk production level per average cow per year was slightly higher in the large-scale farms with high-grade Friesians (2377 kg) than in the (peri-)urban farms with crossbreds (1829 kg), although this dif- ference was not statistically significant. The milk production level in the (peri-)urban farms is comparable to milk production levels of crossbred and European breeds in smallholder farms in other areas in East Africa (Bebe et al., 2002; Weiler et al., 2014). The great majority of the cattle kept on rural farms were cows of local bos indicus breeds. Their average milk production per cow per year (730 kg) was much lower than the average production levels in the other two systems. In rural farms cows are milked with the calf at foot. Farmers allow calves to suckle before

* Cattle unit: lactating cow, dry cow, mature bull = 1; heifer = 0.7; calf = 0.2. Lactating + dry cows. Including hired labour. a,b Means with different superscripts are significantly different at P < 0.05.

and after milking. So, total milk yields will be higher than the milk off- take estimate. In India calf intake was estimated at one third of the milk off-take level (Patil and Udo, 1997). The milk off-take level in rural farms was well above the estimate of, on average, 305 kg per year for local breeds in Ethiopia (Haile, 2009). This overall estimate includes milk production estimates of local cattle kept in the pastoral system, which are very low as shortages of feed and water frequently occur.

Milk was the main contributor to the economic benefits of large- scale (90%) and (peri-)urban (80%) systems (Table 3). Table 3 shows the estimated economic contributions and their wide variation of sold and home consumed milk (range: 20-58%), sale of animals (0-43%), manure (6-16%), insurance value (14-36%), financing value (0-3%) and status value (4-11%) in the rural system. In the rural system milk contributed, on average, only about 40% to the total benefits of cattle keeping. The economic efficiency, measured as GM per cu, was about the same in the large-scale and (peri)-urban system (1008 and 974 US$ y-1). In the rural system the economic efficiency was much lower (360 US$ y-1. The additional benefits of cattle keeping in the rural system could not make up for the lower milk production per cow. In the rural system, the estimated economic value of the manure was equivalent to about 27% of the value of the annual milk production. This was com- parable to the estimate of the value of manure of about one-third of the value of the milk produced in smallholder dairy farms in the Kenya Highlands (Lekasi et al., 2001). Lekasi et al. (2001) concluded that li- vestock are important as nutrient conduits, concentrators and con- verters in rural systems.

38: <header>3.4. Environmental impacts per cu and their hotspots </header>

Table 4 shows the LU, FEU, and GWP per cu for the three cattle keeping systems. Major hotspots of these environmental categories are presented as percentages of the total impacts. The LU per cu was sig- nificantly higher in the large-scale (14 110 m² and (peri-)urban farms (12 480 m² y-1 than in the rural farms (2760 m² y-1. The main hotspot of LU was land used for feed production: roughages (74%, 64% and 81% of the total LU for large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms, respectively) and wheat bran (15%, 26% and 8%, respectively). The use of communal land for grazing was not included in the LU estimate for

the rural farms; consequently the rural system shows a considerably lower LU per animal. The biomass production on the communal grazing areas is very low due to overgrazing and long dry periods. When we use an approximate estimate from literature of an average DM intake per grazing day on communal areas of 2.5 kg per day (Patil and Udo, 1997) and estimates of 700-1000 kg DM per ha per year in communal areas in Ethiopia (Yayneshet et al., 2009; Amsalu and Addisu, 2014) then the LU per cu estimate for the rural system can be increased with about 10-13 000 m² and is comparable to the other two systems.

The FEU per cu did not differ between the large-scale (12 090 MJ y-1 and (peri-)urban farms (13 260 MJ y-1), whereas the rural farms had a significantly lower FEU per cu (2020 MJ y-1). FEU was mainly caused by the transport and processing of wheat bran for the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms (47% and 43% of the total FEU, respectively) and harvesting and transporting roughages for the large-scale, (peri-) urban and rural farms (28%, 30% and 64%, respectively).

The GWP per cu estimates were significantly lower in the rural farms (700 CO2-eq y-1) than in the large-scale (2760 CO2-eq y-1 and (peri-)urban (2710 CO2-eq y-1 farms. About 58%, 47% and 64% of the total GWP for the large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms respectively was due to on-farm emissions from enteric fermentation and manure storage. Other hotspots were transport and processing of wheat bran (20%, 18% and 9% of the total GWP in the large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms, respectively) and cultivation of roughages (15%, 19% and 22% in the large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms, respectively).

43: The LU, FEU and GWP estimates per kg milk did not differ between the three systems (Table 4). The LU values per kg milk of 8.8-11.2m were considerably higher than LU values of milk production in OECD

(Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development) countries which vary from 1.1 to 2.0 m² (De Vries and De Boer, 2010). Main reasons are the use of off-farm forages from land with relatively low biomass production and the large amounts of wheat bran fed in the Mekelle milkshed area. The FEU values of 6.6 11.1 MJ per kg milk, on the other hand, were considerably lower than FEU values of milk pro- duction in OECD countries which vary from 37 to 144 MJ per kg milk (De Vries and De Boer, 2010). Milk production systems in developed countries are highly energy intensive compared to milk production in countries such as Ethiopia.

The large-scale system tended to have a lower GWP per kg milk (1.75 kg CO2-eq) than the (peri-)urban (2.25 kg CO2-eq) and rural systems (2.22 kg CO2-eq), but these differences were not significantly different. The large-scale and (peri-)urban farms had higher milk out- puts per animal than the rural farms but they use much more feed in- puts too. Moreover, cattle on the rural farms had multiple functions, so, GWP was allocated to a greater variety of economic benefits than in the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms. The present GWP values per kg milk in the large-scale, (peri-)urban and rural farms were slightly higher than GWP values for large-scale, medium-scale and smallholder farms in Kenya of 1.1, 1.3 and 1.8 kg CO2-eq respectively (Udo et al., 2016) and 1.7 kg CO2-eq in smallholder farms in India (Garg et al., 2016) or 0.8-1.3 CO2-eq in OECD countries (De Vries and De Boer, 2010). However, GWP per kg milk estimates of milk production in SSA from explorative global studies (7.5 and 9.0 CO2-eq in FAO, 2010; Gerber et al., 2013, respectively) are much higher than our empirical results of the different types of Ethiopian farms.

45: Milk LCA studies are dominated by studies on climate impact per kg milk in western production systems; only few studies have been done in other regions. Added to this, the results of different studies are some- times difficult to compare, as they are conditioned by the practitioners choices (Baldini et al., 2016). The methods of assessing impacts might differ, and allocation of emissions, and land and energy use to only biophysical products or all benefits of cattle keeping will have an effect on the results. In the rural system the impacts per kg milk would have been about 1.5 times higher when these impacts had been allocated to biophysical products only. Our study and the studies in Kenya and India on GWP per kg milk used more or less similar methods of calculating and allocating the environmental impacts. A major reason for the much higher GWP per kg milk estimates of milk production in SSA from ex- plorative global studies compared to our and the Kenyan and Indian case-specific results could be that the global studies modelled average farms with milk production levels of all types of cattle. These produc- tion levels are lower than milk yields of cattle specifically used for dairy production in the Mekelle milkshed area or in smallholder milk pro- duction systems in Kenya and India. Moreover the additional benefits of cattle in rural systems are not included in global studies.

Uncertainties associated with the input data will also affect the re- sults of LCA studies (Wolf et al., 2016). The inventory of the hotspots indicates that the estimates of feed inputs will have had a major impact on the results. Feed use on farms in daily practice is also difficult to properly determine. In particular, our estimates of the use of roughages are rather rough approximations of roughage use. Also, milk production is an essential parameter to assess impacts per kg milk (Wolf et al., 2016). The large-scale farms kept good records of milk production, whereas the milk yields of cows in the (peri-)urban were irregularly kept. So, milk production estimates of (peri-)urban and rural farms were mainly based on recall information from the farmers. Regular monitoring of feeds used at farm level and milk yields of individual cows can reduce uncertainty of estimates of environmental impacts per kg milk, however, this is time-consuming and requires additional re- sources.

48: <header>3.7. Is intensification beneficial for the environment? </header>

Intensification of dairy production via small-scale (peri-)urban farms or large-scale farms resulted in much larger absolute impacts, expressed as LU, FEU and GWP per cu compared to cattle keeping in rural farms (Table 4). The impacts per kg of milk, however, did not differ significantly between the three farm types. So, enhancing in- tensification by promoting a specific farming system is not expected to make a substantial contribution to reducing environmental impacts per kg milk in countries such as Ethiopia.

Milk production per cow present is often used as measurement of intensification level. Fig. 1 shows how milk production per average cow (lactating and dry cows) per year related to the GWP per kg milk. All rural farms had relatively low milk production levels but the GWP per kg milk estimates varied widely (1.0-3.2 kg CO2-eq). The three rural farms with relatively low GWP per kg milk values (Fig. 1) fed hardly any wheat bran, whereas four of the five farms with relatively high GWP per kg milk fed large amounts of wheat bran (15% of total DM fed). The number of farms is small, as LCA is a data-intensive metho- dology. Nevertheless, it seems that the results of the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms are in line with the asymptotic relation of increasing GWP per kg milk with decreasing milk yields as shown by Gerber et al. (2011). The two (peri-)urban farms and the large-scale farm with a relatively high GWP per kg milk had low milk yields per cow. These low yields were mainly caused by relatively large numbers of dry cows. So, also within the large-scale and (peri-)urban systems there are differ- ences in management levels resulting in wide differences in milk pro- duction and in environmental impact per kg milk.

One of the reasons for the promotion of (peri-)urban dairying, which is a zero-grazing system, is the pressure on agricultural land around Mekelle due to urbanisation. Although the farm sizes of the (peri-)urban farms were very small, their LU was not smaller than in the large-scale or rural farms (including the rough estimate for their use of communal lands) due the large contribution of off-farm land use to the final LU estimate.

51: The quest for sustainable livestock intensification, producing more animal source foods but with limited impact on the environment, dominates discussions on the future of livestock in developing countries (Steinfeld et al., 2006; McDermott et al., 2010; Herrero et al., 2013). Sustainable intensification of agriculture is interpreted in different ways, varying from intensification to increase agricultural production which will automatically reduce the environmental impact to emphasis on more resource-efficient production systems and securing livelihoods of farmers (Cook et al., 2015). In Ethiopia, policy papers put emphasis on commercialization of dairying with securing livelihoods and
reducing environmental impacts as spin-offs (Land O'Lakes, 2010; Wouters and van der Lee, 2010; Anonymus, 2011a).

A major constraint for commercialization of dairying is the lack of high quality forages and supplements. The use of wheat bran is not sufficient to correct the low nutrient densities in hay and straws. The use of other better quality feeds is limited to dairy meal and alfalfa in the large-scale system and atella in the (peri-)urban system. Atella is mainly available during ceremonies and festivities (Gebremariam and Belay, 2016). The feed resources used can only maintain the present milk production levels in the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms. The inventory of the hotspots indicated that the large amounts of wheat bran fed in the large-scale and (peri-)urban systems played an im- portant part in all environmental impact categories in these systems, whereas the feeding of hay and straw had major impacts on LU and FEU in all farm types. Hence, more locally produced high quality feeds are needed to both increase milk production levels and to reduce en- vironmental impacts in the large-scale and (peri-)urban farms. These farms are market-oriented; the milk they produce will generate suffi- cient cash (see the economic results, Table 3) to buy better quality feeds. Duncan et al. (2013) suggested that the quality of the con- centrates available, however, might be an issue for increasing milk production levels in Ethiopia.

The milk production per average cow in the rural farms (730 is below milk production levels in rural smallholder farms in neighbouring Kenya (1500 kg (Bebe et al., 2002; Weiler et al., 2014). Besides the quality of the grazing and the feed supplements, a main reason could be the types of cattle kept. Dairy oriented small- holders in the Kenyan Highlands own high-grade European dairy breeds, whereas rural farmers in the Mekelle milkshed area mainly keep local breeds and only a few crossbreds. A change to dairy type European breeds could be an opportunity to increase milk productions levels in the rural system. Crossbreeding for milk has been promoted for decades in Ethiopia, however, the rate of adoption is low (Land O'Lakes, 2010; Duncan et al., 2013; Roschinsky et al., 2015). Several studies have suggested that a major drawback for crossbreeding is the insufficient availability of semen and bulls (Duncan et al., 2013; Roschinsky et al., 2015). Also the lack of better quality feeds limits the uptake of cross- breeding. Rural farmers will not have the cash available to invest in concentrates. Legumes could be an opportunity to improve the feed qualities in rural farms. Research on the potential of legumes in Ethiopia indicates, however, that the number of farms planting legume trees is still low. Major constraints are agronomic problems, land shortage for individual farms, labour shortage, and the fact that legume trees have a low multipurpose value (values beyond feed values and soil rehabilitation) (Mekoya et al., 2008). Crossbreeding will necessitate a change towards more market-oriented cattle keeping in the rural areas (Duncan et al., 2013). The fact that most of the milk produced in rural farms was consumed at home indicates that the milk value chain in the rural areas is still in a rudimentary stage. Cattle keeping in rural households will continue to play an important part in securing liveli- hoods. So, rural farmers will assess possibilities for changes in their cattle keeping in the context of their multiple objectives and limited resources (Udo et al., 2016).

Farmers will need training, knowledge and incentives to put sus- tainable intensification into practice. Experiences in Kenya showed that training sessions and farmer to farmer learning can contribute to im- plementation of more environmental friendly farming practices (Rosenstock et al., 2014). Innovations, such as access to breeding ser- vices, access to better quality feeds, possibilities for growing better quality forages, and access to the milk value chain, need to be tailored to the specific characteristics of the different farming systems.

Besides the quality of the grazing and the feed supplements, a main reason could be the types of cattle kept. Dairy oriented small- holders in the Kenyan Highlands own high-grade European dairy breeds, whereas rural farmers in the Mekelle milkshed area mainly keep local breeds and only a few crossbreds. A change to dairy type European breeds could be an opportunity to increase milk productions levels in the rural system. Crossbreeding for milk has been promoted for decades in Ethiopia, however, the rate of adoption is low (Land O'Lakes, 2010; Duncan et al., 2013; Roschinsky et al., 2015). Several studies have suggested that a major drawback for crossbreeding is the insufficient availability of semen and bulls (Duncan et al., 2013; Roschinsky et al., 2015). Also the lack of better quality feeds limits the uptake of cross- breeding. Rural farmers will not have the cash available to invest in concentrates. Legumes could be an opportunity to improve the feed qualities in rural farms. Research on the potential of legumes in Ethiopia indicates, however, that the number of farms planting legume trees is still low. Major constraints are agronomic problems, land shortage for individual farms, labour shortage, and the fact that legume trees have a low multipurpose value (values beyond feed values and soil rehabilitation) (Mekoya et al., 2008). Crossbreeding will necessitate a change towards more market-oriented cattle keeping in the rural areas (Duncan et al., 2013). The fact that most of the milk produced in rural farms was consumed at home indicates that the milk value chain in the rural areas is still in a rudimentary stage. Cattle keeping in rural households will continue to play an important part in securing liveli- hoods. So, rural farmers will assess possibilities for changes in their cattle keeping in the context of their multiple objectives and limited resources (Udo et al., 2016).

Farmers will need training, knowledge and incentives to put sus- tainable intensification into practice. Experiences in Kenya showed that training sessions and farmer to farmer learning can contribute to im- plementation of more environmental friendly farming practices (Rosenstock et al., 2014). Innovations, such as access to breeding ser- vices, access to better quality feeds, possibilities for growing better quality forages, and access to the milk value chain, need to be tailored to the specific characteristics of the different farming systems.
